finding: 7.1.1 requires `active` to be stored as a path relative to the project root, and 7.2.2 rejects on read any `active` that is absolute or escapes the project root (treated as unset). But 7.1.2 ('tp use <file> writes the path to .tp/local.json:active') never specifies how tp use converts its argument into a project-root-relative path. The argument is naturally interpreted relative to the current working directory, which per 2.2.1 is the discovery anchor and may be any subdirectory (the common case in a 45-chapter monorepo) or an absolute path. A naive implementation that stores the raw argument will, when run from a subdirectory or given an absolute path, write a value that 7.2.2 discards on every subsequent read, silently defeating the pointer tp use just set (degrading to auto-detect plus a dangling-pointer warning).
suggestion: Specify in 7.1.2 that tp use resolves its <file> argument against the current working directory and then rebases it to a path relative to the project root before writing `active`; if the resolved target lies outside the project root, tp use must reject it at write time (exit 1) with a hint, rather than writing a value that read-time validation (7.2.2) will silently discard.

finding: It is unspecified whether `--force` also bypasses the dirty-working-tree refusal. The dirty-tree refusal and the existing-config refusal both exit 4, but 'unless --force' grammatically attaches only to the existing-config clause. Since --force's stated purpose (merge the hoist into the existing workflow block) requires --extract to actually run, a test author cannot tell whether `tp config --extract --force` on a dirty tree with an existing .tp/config.json merges (bypassing the dirty check) or exits 4 (dirty check wins).
suggestion: State explicitly whether --force overrides the dirty-tree gate. Recommended: --force does NOT bypass the dirty-tree refusal (dirty tree → exit 4 regardless of --force), so the tree must be clean before --force can merge.

finding: No schema rule categorizes a KNOWN workflow scalar field whose value has the wrong JSON type in a hand-edited .tp/config.json (e.g. {"workflow":{"review_max_rounds":"eight"}}, checks set to a string, or a checks element missing cmd / not an object). §2.3.4 enumerates type mismatches only for `active`, `defaults` entries, and the `workflow`/`defaults` top-level containers; §2.3.5 covers only values 'outside a documented range' (right type, wrong number); §2.3.6 malformed requires invalid JSON or a non-object top level, which such a file is not. A test author cannot determine whether the field warns-and-ignores (fall back to default), aborts exit 3, or something else.
suggestion: Add a rule: a workflow field whose value has the wrong type (non-numeric for a numeric field, non-array for `checks`, non-object `checks` element, non-string `quality_gate`) is reported as a validation warning on stderr and treated as unset — falling back to the built-in default — parallel to §2.3.4/§2.3.5.
